title: RNNs & Transformers
blurb: Sequence models: vanilla RNNs and their gradient problems, how LSTMs fix them with a cell-state highway, and the Transformer (attention, cross-attention, positional encoding). The newest and increasingly-tested material.

knowledge_points.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
knowledge_points.recap: A vanilla RNN cell is $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1}+W x_t+b)$ — the same weights are reused at every step. Parameters in a cell with $x_t\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$, $h_t\in\mathbb{R}^{m}$: $d\!\cdot\!m + m\!\cdot\!m + m$ (input, recurrent, bias). RNNs suffer **vanishing/exploding gradients** over long sequences, have **few parameters** (limited capacity), and **short-term memory**.

questions.q: For a vanilla RNN $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1}+W x_t+b)$ with $x_t\in\mathbb{R}^3$ and $h_t\in\mathbb{R}^5$, how many parameters are in the cell?
questions.extend: Count the three pieces: input-to-hidden ($d\cdot m$), hidden-to-hidden ($m\cdot m$), and bias ($m$). The recurrent matrix $V$ is square ($m\times m$).

questions.q: Give two drawbacks of RNNs that are NOT the exploding/vanishing gradient problem.
questions.answer: (1) **Too few parameters** — limited capacity (the same small weight set is reused at every timestep). (2) **Short-term memory** — they struggle to learn relationships between tokens that are far apart in the sequence.
questions.extend: Beyond gradient issues, RNNs forget long-range context (short memory) and have limited capacity — both motivate LSTMs and then attention.

questions.q: For $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1}+W x_t+b)$: if $x_t$ and $b$ are the zero vector, is it true that $h_t=h_{t-1}$ for any $h_t$?
questions.answer: **False.** Even with $x_t=0$ and $b=0$, $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1})$ — the multiplication by $V$ and the **non-linearity** $\tanh$ change the value, so in general $h_t\neq h_{t-1}$.
questions.extend: Only if $V$ were identity *and* there were no non-linearity would the state pass through unchanged. The recurrent weight + tanh transform the hidden state every step.

questions.q: RNNs over long sequences exhibit a training issue. What is the problem, and how do you address the vanishing vs exploding cases?
questions.answer:
The problem is **vanishing / exploding gradients** (gradients shrink or blow up as they backprop through many timesteps).
**If vanishing:** switch to an **LSTM** (cell-state gradient highway), or enforce the recurrent matrix eigenvalues ≈ 1.
**If exploding:** apply **gradient clipping**.
questions.extend: Backprop-through-time multiplies many Jacobians → product shrinks (vanish) or grows (explode). Clipping caps explosion; LSTM/gating fixes vanishing.

questions.q: Why does using the *same* weights at every timestep make RNNs prone to vanishing/exploding gradients?
questions.answer: Backpropagation through time repeatedly multiplies by the same recurrent Jacobian (involving $V$). Over $T$ steps this is like raising $V$ to the $T$-th power — eigenvalues $<1$ shrink the gradient toward 0 (vanishing) and eigenvalues $>1$ blow it up (exploding), exponentially in sequence length.
questions.extend: Weight sharing across time = repeated multiplication = exponential growth/decay. Eigenvalues ≈ 1 is the knife-edge that keeps gradients stable.

questions.q: Which statements about RNNs are true?
questions.answer: **True:** RNNs **inherently model sequence order** through recurrence, so they don't need positional encoding; and they **maintain a hidden state updated each timestep** to capture temporal dependencies. **False:** RNNs suffer **both** vanishing *and* exploding gradients; and each output depends on the hidden state, **not only** the current input.
questions.extend: RNNs get order for free (sequential processing) — unlike Transformers which need positional encoding.

options.text: During training, RNNs suffer from vanishing gradients, but not exploding gradients.
The output of an RNN at each timestep depends only on the current input.
RNNs inherently model sequence order through recurrence, so they need no positional encoding.
RNNs maintain a hidden state updated each timestep to capture temporal dependencies.

questions.q: Write the (activation-free) RNN cell-state update $C(t)$, then unroll it to show $C(t)$ depends on all previous inputs.
questions.answer: $C(t)=W_c\,C(t{-}1)+W_x X_t$. Unrolling: $C(t)=\sum_{i=0}^{t} W_c^{\,t-i} W_x X_i = W_c^t W_x X_0 + \dots + W_x X_t$ — every past input $X_i$ contributes, weighted by $W_c^{\,t-i}$.
questions.extend: The $W_c^{\,t-i}$ factor is exactly why gradients vanish ($\|W_c\|<1$) or explode ($>1$) over long sequences.

questions.q: An RNN does well on short sentences but worse on long ones. (a) Name the phenomenon. (b) Name a major cause.
questions.answer: (a) **Loss of long-term memory / vanishing (or exploding) gradients.** (b) Old input embeddings are **multiplied by the weight matrix repeatedly** (and passed through the activation many times), so their influence shrinks (or blows up) over many steps.
questions.extend: Repeated multiplication by $W_c$ across timesteps = exponential decay/growth → short memory. LSTMs add a cell-state highway to fix it.

questions.q: When training an RNN, the training loss doesn't decrease. What problem with RNNs could cause this?
questions.answer: **Vanishing or exploding gradients.** Repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight across timesteps makes gradients **shrink toward 0** (no learning) or **blow up** (unstable), so the loss fails to decrease.
questions.extend: Same recurrent weight applied every step ⇒ exponential decay/growth of gradients over the sequence.

questions.q: For a simplified linear RNN $h_t=W_h h_{t-1}+W_x x_t$ with scalar states and $o=h_n$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial o}=1$: (7.2) derive $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_x}$ for $n=3$; (7.3) give the general-$n$ formula; (7.4) for which $W_h$ does the problem occur, the multi-dim condition, the problematic term, and an easy fix that doesn't change the architecture.
questions.answer: Since $\frac{\partial L}{\partial o}=1$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_x}=\frac{\partial h_n}{\partial W_x}$. **(7.2) $n=3$:** $\;x_3+W_h x_2+W_h^2 x_1$. **(7.3) general:** $\;\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_x}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}W_h^{\,n-k}x_k=x_n+W_h x_{n-1}+\dots+W_h^{\,n-1}x_1$. **(7.4)** vanishing if $|W_h|<1$, exploding if $|W_h|>1$; multi-dim: the largest **$|$eigenvalue$|$** of $W_h$ is $<1$ or $>1$; the problematic part is the **exponential term $W_h^{\,n-1}$**; an easy fix is **gradient clipping** (for the exploding case).
questions.extend: The $W_h^{\,n-k}$ factors are exactly the source of vanishing/exploding gradients; LSTMs add a cell-state highway, clipping caps explosions.

questions.q: Name one advantage and one disadvantage of Recurrent Neural Networks in general.
questions.answer: **Advantage (any):** handles **sequential data of arbitrary length**, can model any output / reuse past output information, lightweight (shared weights). **Disadvantage (any):** **vanishing/exploding gradients**, no long-term memory, hard to **parallelize**, long training time.
questions.extend: RNN trades flexible variable-length sequence modeling for unstable gradients and poor parallelism.

questions.q: A vanilla RNN cell is $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1}+W x_t)$ with $x_t\in\mathbb{R}^4,h_t\in\mathbb{R}^{12}$. How many parameters are in the cell (neglect bias)?
questions.answer: $W$ is $12\times4$ and $V$ is $12\times12$, so $4\cdot12+12\cdot12=48+144=\mathbf{192}$ parameters (add $+12$ if a bias is included).
questions.extend: RNN cell params $= h(x+h)$ (+bias); the $h\times h$ recurrent matrix dominates.

questions.q: For $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1}+W x_t)$, is it true that if $x_t$ is the zero vector then $h_t=h_{t-1}$?
questions.answer: **False (in general).** With $x_t=0$, $h_t=\tanh(V h_{t-1})$, which is **not** equal to $h_{t-1}$ unless $h_{t-1}$ happens to be a fixed point of $\tanh(V\cdot)$ (e.g. $h_{t-1}=0$). The hidden state still transforms through $V$ and the non-linearity.
questions.extend: Zero input ≠ identity update: $V$ and $\tanh$ still act on the previous hidden state.

questions.q: A 1-D ReLU-RNN is $h_t=\text{ReLU}(V h_{t-1}+W x_t)$ with $V=1,W=2,h_0=-3,x_1=1,x_2=2,x_3=0$. Compute $h_1,h_2,h_3$.
questions.answer: $h_1=\text{ReLU}(1\cdot({-}3)+2\cdot1)=\text{ReLU}({-}1)=0$; $h_2=\text{ReLU}(0+2\cdot2)=4$; $h_3=\text{ReLU}(4+0)=4$.
questions.extend: ReLU clips the negative first step to 0, then the state accumulates the inputs.

questions.q: Which statements about RNNs are correct?
questions.answer: **True:** RNNs *process sequences sequentially, one time step at a time*, and *share parameters across time steps*. **False:** RNNs **cannot** be fully parallelized over the sequence length, and they are **not** well-suited to very long-range dependencies (vanishing gradients).
questions.extend: RNN = sequential + weight-shared across time; poor at long-range, not parallelizable over length.

options.text: RNNs can be fully parallelized over the sequence length during training.
RNNs process sequences sequentially, one time step at a time.
RNNs are well-suited for modeling very long-range dependencies.
RNNs share parameters across time steps.

questions.q: A 1-D ReLU-RNN $h_t=\text{ReLU}(V h_{t-1}+W x_t)$ with $V=-3,W=3,h_0=0,x_1=2,x_2=3,x_3=1$. Compute $h_2$ and $h_3$.
questions.answer: $h_1=\text{ReLU}(-3\cdot0+3\cdot2)=6$; $h_2=\text{ReLU}(-3\cdot6+3\cdot3)=\text{ReLU}(-9)=0$; $h_3=\text{ReLU}(-3\cdot0+3\cdot1)=3$.
questions.extend: The negative recurrent weight + ReLU clipping makes the state jump to 0 then recover from the input.

questions.q: ReLU-RNN $h_t=\text{ReLU}(V h_{t-1}+W x_t)$ with $V=-2,W=1,h_0=2,x_1=2,x_2=3,x_3=4$ and forward outputs $h_1=0,h_2=\tfrac23,h_3=1$. Compute $\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial V},\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial W},\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial x_1}$ (use $\text{ReLU}'(0)=0$).
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial V}=h_2+V\frac{\partial h_2}{\partial V}=\tfrac23+V\cdot0=\tfrac23$. $\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial W}=V x_2+x_3=-2(\tfrac32)+4=1$. $\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial x_1}=0$ — the gradient path is killed by the **dead ReLU** at $h_1=0$.
questions.extend: BPTT chains the recurrence; a ReLU that output 0 zeroes every gradient flowing through it (dead path).

questions.q: An RNN's training loss won't drop. (9.2) Explain why this happens. (9.3) Name one solution.
questions.answer: **9.2** The **shared recurrent weights are multiplied repeatedly** across time steps; if their eigenvalues are $<1$ the gradient **vanishes**, if $>1$ it **explodes** — so the loss stops improving. **9.3** For vanishing: switch to an **LSTM** (or constrain eigenvalues ≈1); for exploding: **gradient clipping**.
questions.extend: Repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight → vanishing/exploding; LSTM or clipping fixes it.

questions.q: For an RNN-based translation model: (1) what are the input and output of the RNN block? (2) For full-sentence translation, why is a sequence-to-sequence (encode-all-then-decode) pipeline best?
questions.answer: **(1)** Input: the current **word embedding** plus the **previous hidden state**; output: the **current hidden state** (and a per-step prediction). **(2)** Translation needs the **whole input sentence before producing output** (word order/meaning can depend on later words), so an encode-then-decode (seq2seq) structure fits — process all inputs, then generate the output sequence.
questions.extend: RNN block: (embedding, prev state) → new state; translation needs full-input encoding before decoding.

knowledge_points.title: LSTM
knowledge_points.recap: An LSTM adds a **cell state** $C_t$ — a gradient highway analogous to skip connections — plus gates (forget, input, output) using **sigmoid** to decide what to keep/add/output. This lets it carry long-range information and largely solves the vanishing-gradient problem of vanilla RNNs.

questions.q: How did the LSTM solve the vanishing-gradient problem of RNNs?
questions.answer: By introducing the **cell state** — a **highway for gradients** (just like skip connections in ResNets). Information (and gradients) can flow along the cell state across many timesteps with minimal attenuation, so long-range dependencies are preserved.
questions.extend: Same core trick as ResNet's $+1$: an additive, near-identity path for gradients. The gates control what enters/leaves this highway.

questions.q: Is it a good idea to use ReLU instead of Sigmoid as the activation of the LSTM input gate?
questions.answer: **No.** The **sigmoid** is bounded in $[0,1]$, so it acts as a **gate** deciding *what fraction* of information to pass (0 = block, 1 = pass). **ReLU** is unbounded above, which destroys this gating behaviour and can cause **numerical instabilities**.
questions.extend: Gates need a $[0,1]$ 'valve' → sigmoid. Candidate values (what to write) use tanh. ReLU's unbounded range breaks the valve metaphor.

questions.q: Name the three gates of an LSTM and what each controls.
questions.answer: **Forget gate** — how much of the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ to keep. **Input gate** — how much of the new candidate information to write into the cell state. **Output gate** — how much of the cell state to expose as the hidden state $h_t$. All three use sigmoids to produce $[0,1]$ gating values.
questions.extend: Mnemonic: forget (erase old), input (write new), output (read out). The cell state is updated as $C_t = f\odot C_{t-1} + i\odot \tilde C_t$.

questions.q: Which statements correctly describe the gating mechanisms in an LSTM?
questions.answer: **True:** the gates **allow the network to forget or retain information over long sequences**. **False:** gates are not there to limit parameters, not to reduce hidden-state dimensionality over time, and not to make the output depend only on the most recent token.
questions.extend: Gates (forget/input/output) regulate the cell-state highway — what to keep, write, and read.

options.text: To limit the number of parameters in the model.
To allow the network to forget or retain information over long sequences.
To progressively reduce the dimensionality of the hidden state over time.
To ensure the output always depends only on the most recent input token.

questions.q: Explain the role of the sigmoid activation in an LSTM unit.
questions.answer: The sigmoid acts as a **gate**: in the input/forget/output gates it outputs values in $[0,1]$ — like a soft switch — deciding **how much information to keep or forget**.
questions.extend: Sigmoid ∈ [0,1] = a valve; that's why ReLU (unbounded) is a poor gate activation.

questions.q: In LSTMs, why do we apply Tanh to the cell state before using it (1p), but sigmoid for the gates (1p)?
questions.answer: **Tanh** bounds the cell-state values to $[-1,1]$, **preventing uncontrolled growth** over long sequences. **Sigmoid** (range $[0,1]$) makes the gates act as **soft switches**, controlling **how much information flows through** (forget / write / read fractions).
questions.extend: Tanh = bounded content; sigmoid = a valve in $[0,1]$. That's why ReLU (unbounded) is a poor gate activation.

questions.q: Explain how the three gates in an LSTM control the cell state.
questions.answer: **Forget gate:** decides how much of the previous (long-term) cell state to **discard**. **Input gate:** controls how much **new information is written** into the cell state. **Output gate:** controls how much of the cell state is **exposed as the output** / short-term hidden state.
questions.extend: Forget→erase, input→write, output→read — three sigmoidal valves regulating the cell-state highway.

questions.q: Describe gradient clipping (2p) and explain why it particularly benefits RNN training (1p).
questions.answer: **Gradient clipping** caps the magnitude (norm or value) of gradients during backprop to a threshold, preventing them from becoming **excessively large**. RNNs are especially prone to **exploding gradients** because the same recurrent weight is applied across many time steps, so clipping keeps their training **stable**.
questions.extend: Clipping bounds the gradient norm — the standard fix for RNN exploding gradients (not vanishing).

questions.q: An LSTM is $g_1,g_2,g_3=\sigma(\cdot)$, $\tilde c_t=\tanh(\cdot)$, $c_t=g_2\circ c_{t-1}+g_3\circ\tilde c_t$, $h_t=g_1\circ c_t$. (1) Assign $g_1,g_2,g_3$ to the forget/input/output gates. (2) What does $c_t$ represent?
questions.answer: $g_2$ (multiplies the previous cell state) = **forget gate**; $g_3$ (multiplies the candidate) = **input/update gate**; $g_1$ (multiplies $c_t$ to form $h_t$) = **output gate**. $c_t$ = the **cell state / long-term memory**.
questions.extend: Read the gates from the equations: forget scales $c_{t-1}$, input scales $\tilde c_t$, output scales $c_t$ into $h_t$.

questions.q: Explain how LSTMs often outperform traditional RNNs. What in their architecture enables this?
questions.answer: Traditional RNNs struggle to learn **long-term dependencies** because of **vanishing gradients**. The LSTM's **cell state** provides a near-additive path that **improves gradient flow** across many time steps, letting the network retain information over long sequences.
questions.extend: The cell-state highway is the LSTM's key fix for the RNN vanishing-gradient/long-memory problem.

questions.q: (1) What activations are used for an LSTM's forget gate, input gate, candidate memory, and output gate? (2) Is ReLU a good choice for the forget gate? (3) Why is LSTM better on long sequences?
questions.answer: **(1)** **Sigmoid** for the three gates (forget/input/output); **tanh** for the candidate memory. **(2)** **No** — ReLU's output can exceed 1, so it can't act as a $[0,1]$ gate and could make the cell state grow unbounded. **(3)** The **cell state provides a highway** for long-term dependencies (near-additive gradient flow).
questions.extend: Gates = sigmoid (valves in [0,1]); candidate = tanh; cell-state highway handles long-range memory.

knowledge_points.title: Transformers: Attention & Architecture
knowledge_points.recap: Transformers replace recurrence with **attention**. Self-attention computes $\text{softmax}\!\big(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big)V$. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling and **layer normalization** (before/after attention blocks) keep gradients stable. **Cross-attention** in the decoder uses queries from the decoder and keys/values from the encoder. Predicting the next token is a **classification** task (one label per vocabulary word).

questions.q: Why is the self-attention mechanism prone to exploding gradients, and how does the original Transformer architecture solve it?
questions.answer: Self-attention itself has **no normalization** besides the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ scale, so the affine layers can produce **very large values → huge gradients**. The Transformer adds **layer normalization** (around the attention and feed-forward blocks), normalizing each embedding individually to keep activations/gradients stable.
questions.extend: Two stabilizers: the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scale on the scores, and LayerNorm + residual connections around each sub-block. LayerNorm (per-token) suits variable-length sequences better than BatchNorm.

questions.q: Cross-attention: given encoder outputs $X_e\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times M}$ and decoder outputs $X_d\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times M}$, what is the output dimension? Is predicting the next token classification or regression?
questions.answer:
Use $q=X_d$, $k=X_e$, $v=X_e$: $\text{out}=(q\,k^\top)\,v \Rightarrow (K\times M)(M\times N)(N\times M)=(K\times N)(N\times M)=\mathbf{K\times M}$.
**Next-token prediction is classification** — each word in the vocabulary is its own class.
questions.extend: Cross-attention lets each decoder position ($K$) attend over all encoder positions ($N$); output keeps the decoder length $K$. Language modeling = classification over the vocabulary.

questions.q: Can a Transformer take embeddings of different sizes? Can it take sequences of different lengths at the encoder/decoder?
questions.answer:
**Embedding size:** **No** — the internal fully-connected layers expect a **fixed embedding dimension**.
**Sequence length:** **Yes** — sequences of embeddings are like batches of tokens, and the FC layers handle different batch/sequence sizes; cross-attention also handles differing encoder/decoder lengths.
questions.extend: Fixed feature width, flexible sequence length — the opposite of FC-based CNNs (fixed spatial size). Attention is permutation/length-agnostic by construction.

questions.q: Why is the dot-product attention score scaled by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ before the softmax?
questions.answer: For large key dimension $d_k$, dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing softmax into **saturated** regions with tiny gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the scores at a reasonable scale, so softmax gradients stay healthy.
questions.extend: The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scale stabilizes attention; combined with LayerNorm it prevents exploding gradients.

questions.q: Which statements about Transformers are true?
questions.answer: **True:** the **attention mechanism itself is invariant to order**; and due to **masked attention**, the decoder output depends only on previous outputs and the encoder. **False:** Transformers apply beyond text (vision, audio), and they do **not** rely on convolutional layers for context.
questions.extend: Self-attention is permutation-invariant (hence positional encoding); causal masking enforces left-to-right decoding.

options.text: The attention mechanism itself is invariant to order.
The concept of a transformer can only be applied to text data.
Transformers utilize convolutional layers to gather context information.
Due to masked attention, the decoder output depends only on previous outputs and the encoder.

questions.q: Why are Transformers better suited than RNNs for GPU parallelism, and why do they capture long-range dependencies well?
questions.answer: **Parallelism:** self-attention computes interactions between **all tokens simultaneously**, so the sequence is processed in parallel, whereas RNNs are **sequential** (each step waits for the previous one). **Long-range:** every token can **directly attend to every other token**, regardless of distance, so far-apart dependencies are captured in one step (no multi-step decay).
questions.extend: Attention = parallel + direct token-to-token links; RNN = sequential + distance-decayed memory.

questions.q: In $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}\!\big(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt d}\big)V$ with $Q,K,V\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}$, is softmax applied over rows or columns? Why?
questions.answer: Over the **rows** of the attention matrix. Each query's output is a **weighted sum of values**, and those weights must **sum to 1** — softmax over the row (across all keys) normalizes the weights for that query.
questions.extend: Row-wise softmax ⇒ each query distributes attention (summing to 1) over all keys.

questions.q: Transformers stack many attention layers and can suffer vanishing gradients. (a) What fix (borrowed from deep CNNs) helps? (b) Is the RNN vanishing-gradient cause the same?
questions.answer: (a) **Residual / skip connections** around each sub-block. (b) **No** — Transformer vanishing comes from the **number of layers (depth)**, whereas RNN vanishing comes from the **length of the input sequence** (repeated recurrence).
questions.extend: Same cure (residuals), different cause: depth (Transformer) vs sequence length (RNN).

questions.q: Which statements about self-attention / Transformers are true?
questions.answer: **True:** self-attention is permutation-invariant (hence positional encoding), scores are scaled by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$, and any token can attend directly to any other. **False:** Transformers do **not** process tokens sequentially like RNNs — they run in parallel.
questions.extend: Attention: order-agnostic (needs positional encoding), $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling, all-to-all, fully parallel.

options.text: Self-attention is permutation-invariant unless positional encoding is added.
Transformers process tokens sequentially, one at a time, like RNNs.
The $QK^\top$ scores are scaled by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ for stability.
Attention lets any token attend directly to any other token.

questions.q: Given $Q,K,V$ of shape $N\times C$, write the formula of (scaled dot-product) attention.
questions.answer: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}\!\Big(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{C}}\Big)V$. The softmax is taken **over the rows** (each query over all keys), and the scores are divided by $\sqrt{C}$ (the feature/channel dimension).
questions.extend: Memorize the exact form: $K$ transposed, divide by $\sqrt{C}$, softmax inside, $V$ outside.

questions.q: What is the role of dividing by the channel dimension ($\sqrt{C}$) in the attention formula?
questions.answer: It **scales down the dot-product scores** (which grow with the dimension $C$) so the softmax input stays in a sensible range → **numerical stability**, avoiding an overly **peaked softmax** and the resulting **small / vanishing gradients**.
questions.extend: Without $1/\sqrt{C}$, large-$C$ dot products saturate the softmax (near one-hot) and gradients vanish.

questions.q: Why does the self-attention mechanism become challenging for extremely long sequences?
questions.answer: It requires a **quadratic amount of memory and compute, $O(n^2)$**, in the sequence length $n$ — every token attends to every other token — so cost blows up for long sequences.
questions.extend: The $n\times n$ attention matrix is the bottleneck; efficient-attention variants approximate it to scale.

questions.q: Multi-head attention with embedding dim 8, 3 heads, $Q/K$ dim 4, $V$ dim 6, weights multiplied from the right ($Y=XW$), output dimension must match the input for the residual. Give the shape of each weight matrix (use $k\times(n\times m)$ for per-head weights).
questions.answer: $W_q:\ 3\times(8\times4)$, $W_k:\ 3\times(8\times4)$, $W_v:\ 3\times(8\times6)$, $W_o:\ (18\times8)$. Each head projects the 8-dim embedding to its $Q/K$ (4) or $V$ (6); the three heads' value outputs concatenate to $3\cdot6=18$, then $W_o$ maps $18\to8$ to restore the residual dimension.
questions.extend: Per-head projections are not shared (hence $3\times$); the output projection $W_o$ merges concatenated heads back to the model dim.

questions.q: In a Transformer for machine translation (predicting the next word, full input+output sentence given), why must we mask the outputs fed into the decoder?
questions.answer: To **mask out future information**: the decoder must predict the next word using only the **already-generated (past) words and the encoder output**. Without masking the model could **'cheat'** by attending to / copying the future ground-truth tokens.
questions.extend: Causal (look-ahead) masking enforces autoregressive, left-to-right decoding so the model can't see the answer.

questions.q: Transition from RNNs to Transformers: (1) why can a Transformer train much faster than an RNN on the same hardware? (2) Compare the 'path length' (sequential ops for info to travel between two distant tokens) of an RNN vs a Transformer.
questions.answer: **(1) Parallelization:** RNNs process tokens **sequentially**; Transformers have **no recurrence** and process **all tokens simultaneously**. **(2) Path length:** RNN path length is **$O(n)$** (linear in sequence length); Transformer path length is **constant $O(1)$** (any two tokens attend directly).
questions.extend: Attention = parallel + constant path length; recurrence = sequential + linear path length.

questions.q: How are Q, K, V generated in self-attention vs cross-attention blocks (ignore multi-head)?
questions.answer: **Self-attention:** Q, K, V come from **three separate linear layers applied to the same input**. **Cross-attention:** Q and K are linear projections of one input ($a$, e.g. the decoder/encoder output) while **V is a projection of the other input** ($b$) — letting one sequence query another.
questions.extend: Self-attn: one source for Q,K,V. Cross-attn: queries from one sequence, keys/values from another.

questions.q: Why do Transformers use multiple attention heads rather than a single larger head?
questions.answer: Multiple heads attend to **different representation subspaces / relationships** in parallel, increasing expressiveness — each head can capture a distinct type of dependency that a single head would average away.
questions.extend: Multi-head = several parallel attention 'views' → richer relational modeling than one big head.

questions.q: In a Transformer, what are the purposes of self- vs cross-attention, and which blocks use masking and why?
questions.answer: **Self-attention:** gathers information **within** a sequence (encoder over input, decoder over generated output). **Cross-attention:** lets the **decoder query the encoder's input** sequence. **Masking** is used in the **decoder self-attention** to **prevent attending to future tokens** (no 'cheating' / look-ahead during training).
questions.extend: Self-attn = intra-sequence, cross-attn = decoder→encoder; causal mask keeps decoding autoregressive.

questions.q: (9.4) In a translation Transformer fed the full input+output sentence, why must the decoder outputs be masked? (9.5) Can a Transformer take an input of arbitrary length? Explain.
questions.answer: **9.4** Because the **entire output sentence is provided**, the decoder must **mask out the tokens after the current position** so it can't 'see' the future word it is supposed to predict (no cheating). **9.5** **No** — the attention layer's $K,Q,V$ projections use **fixed-size matrices**, so the input length is bounded by the fixed configuration.
questions.extend: Causal masking hides future tokens; fixed-size attention projections cap the sequence length.

questions.q: Which layers are used in a Transformer model?
questions.answer: **Fully connected layers** (the position-wise feed-forward networks and Q/K/V projections) and **Softmax layers** (inside attention). **Recurrent** and **convolutional** layers are **not** part of a standard Transformer.
questions.extend: Transformers = attention (softmax) + feed-forward (FC); no recurrence or convolution.

options.text: Recurrent layers
Fully connected layers
Softmax layers
Convolutional layers

questions.q: Transformers for translation: (1) how to inform the model of word order? (2) how are attention weights computed (and their characteristics)? (3) how is the attention output computed, and why is it called 'attention'?
questions.answer: **(1) Positional encoding** added to the token embeddings. **(2)** Weights = **softmax of the scaled dot products $QK^\top$**; the closer a query is to a key, the **larger the weight** (weights are non-negative and sum to 1). **(3)** Output = **weighted sum of the Values** using those weights; it's called 'attention' because the output is dominated by the values with **large weights** (the tokens it 'attends to').
questions.extend: PE supplies order; softmax(QKᵀ) gives the weights; output = weighted sum of V.

knowledge_points.title: Positional Encoding
knowledge_points.recap: Self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — the scores don't depend on token order. To inject order, Transformers add **positional encodings** to the input embeddings, unlike RNNs which process tokens sequentially and get order for free.

questions.q: Why is positional encoding important in Transformers, in comparison to RNNs?
questions.answer: Transformers do **not** process the sequence sequentially — the attention scores between two embeddings are **invariant to token order (permutation-invariant)**. So we must add **positional encoding** to inject time/order information. RNNs get this for free because they consume tokens one-by-one in order.
questions.extend: No recurrence ⇒ no inherent order ⇒ add positional encodings. Without them, 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would look identical to attention.

questions.q: In the Transformer model, why is positional encoding added to the input embeddings?

options.text: To reduce the dimensionality of the input embeddings.
To introduce information about the order of tokens in the sequence.
To improve the accuracy of the attention mechanism.
To make the embeddings compatible with the feed-forward network.

questions.answer: Positional encoding **introduces information about the order of tokens**. Self-attention alone is order-agnostic, so without positional encoding the model couldn't tell sequence order.
questions.extend: It encodes *where* each token sits; it doesn't change dimensionality or directly 'boost accuracy'. Order is the whole point.

questions.q: To feed a CNN feature map ($B\times C\times H\times W$) into a Transformer, how do you turn pixels into tokens?
questions.answer: Move channels last and flatten the spatial dims: $(B\times C\times H\times W)\to(B\times H\times W\times C)\to(B\times(H\cdot W)\times C)$. Each pixel becomes a $C$-dimensional **token**, giving a sequence of $H\cdot W$ tokens per image.
questions.extend: This 'flatten spatial → tokens' reshape is the bridge from CNNs to Vision Transformers (cross-listed from Ch III).

questions.q: 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' contain the same tokens. Without positional encoding, would self-attention distinguish them? Why?
questions.answer: **No.** Self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — scores depend only on token content, not order — so both yield the same set of representations. **Positional encodings** inject order so the model can tell them apart.
questions.extend: Attention has no built-in notion of order; positional encodings supply it (RNNs get order from sequential processing).

questions.q: Why is positional encoding used in the transformer architecture for sequential data (1p)? What problem arises without it (2p)?
questions.answer: It **adds information about each token's position** in the sequence. **Without it**, self-attention treats the input as an **unordered set** — there is no inherent notion of order — yet **order changes meaning** ('dog bites man' ≠ 'man bites dog'), so the model couldn't distinguish reorderings.
questions.extend: No recurrence/convolution ⇒ no built-in order ⇒ positional encoding supplies it.
